examples.title: Linear Search in Array
examples.description: Search for an element by checking each element sequentially
examples.explanation: Linear search compares each element with the target key until found or array ends. Simple but inefficient for large arrays.

examples.title: Binary Search in Sorted Array
examples.description: Search in sorted array by repeatedly dividing search interval in half
examples.explanation: Binary search works on sorted arrays by halving the search space in each iteration. Requires sorted input.

examples.title: Insertion in Sorted Array
examples.description: Insert element at correct position maintaining sorted order
examples.explanation: Finds correct position by comparing elements, shifts larger elements right, then inserts. Maintains sorted order.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) in worst case (insert at beginning)

examples.title: Dynamic Array Implementation
examples.description: Implementation showing how dynamic arrays handle automatic resizing
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DynamicArrayInit
BEGIN
  capacity = INITIAL_CAPACITY (e.g., 4)
  size = 0
  arr = allocate(capacity)
END

ALGORITHM Append(arr, element)
BEGIN
  IF size == capacity THEN
    // Resize: double the capacity
    newCapacity = capacity * 2
    newArr = allocate(newCapacity)
    
    // Copy all elements
    FOR i FROM 0 TO size-1 DO
      newArr[i] = arr[i]
    END FOR
    
    arr = newArr
    capacity = newCapacity
  END IF
  
  arr[size] = element
  size = size + 1
END

// C++ vector uses similar approach
// Java ArrayList doubles capacity when load factor exceeded
examples.explanation: Dynamic arrays automatically resize when full. Typical implementation doubles capacity, giving amortized O(1) append time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized, O(n) worst case during resize
